Before time was measured in hours, the Moon was a perfectly smooth orb of glowing alabaster. She was the pride of the night sky, casting a gentle, unbroken, and flawless light upon the sleeping Earth. The stars, scattered and small, grew fiercely jealous of her undeniable beauty. They whispered ancient grievances to the dark void, conspiring to tarnish her perfection.

One crisp, silent night, when the Sun was deep in his slumber beneath the horizon, the stars rebelled. They gathered cosmic dust and frost, forging them into jagged stones of burning ice, and hurled them across the heavens at the Moon.

The Earth below lay vulnerable, filled with fragile new life. Seeing the barrage of meteoric stones falling toward the world, the benevolent Moon made a sacrifice. She threw herself in the path of the celestial assault, acting as a great silver shield. The stones slammed into her pristine surface, carving deep, permanent scars and jagged craters into her face.

When the Sun finally rose, he wept at her battered state. Yet the Earth rejoiced in her bravery. To this day, the Moon wears her craters not as blemishes, but as eternal medals of her unwavering love and protection.

Myth: After the jealous stars hurled their jagged stones of burning ice, the Moon hung in the sky, battered and scarred. He knew the Moon could not bear the constant, glaring scrutiny of the cosmos while she healed.

To offer her solace, the Sun gathered the deepest shadows from the edges of the void—a quiet, velvet darkness—and wove them into a magical cloak. He draped this cloak over the Moon, hiding her from the arrogant stars. 

But the Earth, who owed its life to her sacrifice, missed her gentle silver light. Hearing the Earth's sorrowful cries, the Moon and Sun agreed on a compromise. Slowly, night by night, the Moon pulls back her cloak of shadows to reveal her face, sharing her light with the world below. Once a month, she stands fully unwrapped, proudly displaying her scars as eternal medals of protection, before drawing the quiet dark around herself once more to rest. Thus, the lunar phases were born.
